entry.title: webhook in cdc
entry.content:
Hello All,

I have created a parent site and it has for now a single child site attached to it. I have created all the ui builder screen set changes, hosted pages, email template related changes in Parent site. All these changes are inherited by the child site as well. Also, in addition to this i have created a password change webhook at Parent site.

Now when my event is getting fired for a webhook, but it is giving below error. In local it is working fine because there is no parent child site but in higher environment facing this issue. Can someone guide me here please.

Checked the flow where the jwt is failing there it is fetching the API key from the gigyaConfig( which in my case i have given of the child). But in my webhook payload request it is of Parent's site as Webhook is created at parent level.

  1. Can you help me in understanding how the config model should be configured when there are parent child sites involved? 
  2. If in future one more child gets attached to this parent what gigyaConfig should be configured?
  3. Why  this webhook is not working ?

entry.title: Chart of Accounts strategy for multi-country group implementation — which approach is recommended?
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

We are implementing SAP FI for a group headquartered in Country A (HQ), with 3 Company Codes located in:

  • Country B 
  • Country C 
  • Country D 

Each subsidiary operates under a different local accounting standard. We are trying to decide on the right Chart of Accounts (COA) architecture and would appreciate feedback from consultants who have implemented similar multi-country scenarios.

We have identified 4 possible approaches:

  1. Single unified Operational COA for the whole group (no Country COA) simple, but doesn't natively support each country's local legal/fiscal reporting requirements.
  2. Local Operational COA per country + Group COA for consolidation (using the Alternative Account Number field)  each Company Code posts directly in its local GAAP, and the Group COA handles consolidation at HQ level.
  3. Unified Operational COA for daily group reporting + local Country COA per subsidiary for legal/fiscal compliance daily postings use the group-harmonized COA, and a Country Specific COA (mapped via Alternative Account Number) is used for statutory/tax reporting.
  4. All three COA types simultaneously (Operational + Group + Country) maximum coverage but heavier configuration and maintenance.

Our questions:

  • Which of these architectures is most commonly recommended for a group with 3+ subsidiaries under different local GAAPs?
  • In practice, does the end user post a single journal entry, with SAP handling the account mapping automatically via the Alternative Account Number field (OB13/OB62 configuration)? Or are there cases requiring separate postings?
  • Are there real-world limitations or pitfalls with Approach 2 or 3 when it comes to VAT reporting, financial statement versions (FSV), or statutory audit file requirements?
  • Any SAP Notes, Best Practices documentation, or real implementation experience you could share would be very helpful.

Thank you in advance for your insights!
SAP S/4HANA Finance  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Finance  SAP S/4HANA Finance for group reporting

entry.title: Re: Statutory Payment Remittance issue - Document type &#...
entry.content:
Good day @Palaksha_SA 

Thank you for your question

 

Your understanding is correct. In Public cloud, you cannot implement this condition directly in the payment format tree when Document type, Item Text, and Reference Document Number are not displayed in the delivered payment format source structure.

The required logic is:

If document type = YA

<Ustrd> = SGTXT

Else

<Ustrd> = XBLNR

The solution provided is for you to calculate the final value through the released BAdI payment Medium: Custom Additional Reference fields. This BAdI is provided specifically for filling up to ten customer reference fields for payment generation. Each field can contain up to 132 characters, and you can then map them into their payment file.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/fd7d1ad757f14d98933c87c9cd259418.html?locale=en-US

Here are preferred implementations:

In Custom logic, you should search for Payment Medium: Custom Additional Reference Fields and create an implementation for the relevant payment process.

Ensure you use only the document and payment item data displayed by BAdI interface or released CDS views.

It is important you fill one custom reference field with the final remittance value.

In general:

IF accountingdocumenttype = ‘YA’.

Customreferencefield1 = accountingdocumentitemtext

ELSE

Customreferencefield1 = referencedocument.

ENDIF.

You should take the exact parameter names from the BAdI interface in your system. Note that direct access to technical table fields such as BSEG-SGTXT or BKPF – XBLNR must not be assumed.

After you have published the implementation, open the configuration activity Map Format Data for Payments, activity ID 100558. This activity controls how the system data is mapped into the final outgoing XML or flat payment file.

 

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/656a410b5ce14bcd82d40b88f26d8d74/6efd7791bf4d4be7b5a961861baad186.html?locale=en-US

It is important you copy the system’s delivered mapping, locate the <Ustrd> node, and assign the custom reference field to it. Custom reference fields filled by BAdI can subsequently be mapped through this configuration activity.

Consult the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/fd7d1ad757f14d98933c87c9cd259418.html?locale=en-US

You can test with:

  • One cleared invoice using document type YA, where <Ustrd> must contain the item text
  • One invoice using another document type, where <Ustrd> must contain the reference document.

 

 Let me know if you have any further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: The Octopus Brain
entry.content:
An octopus has around 500 million neurons in a massive nervous system. Where humans concentrate most neurons in the head, an octopus does the opposite. About two-thirds live in the arms themselves — roughly 40 million per arm — forming eight semi-autonomous mini-brains. Each arm tastes, touches, and makes motor decisions on its own. A central brain coordinates.
That's the architecture I ended up building — accidentally, then deliberately — for the way Claude works with my knowledge. Hence: Project Octopus. Here's what broke, what fixed it, and a prompt you can borrow to try the same pattern on your own knowledge bases.

The problem: four brains, no conversation

The whole thing started after I worked through Ken Lomax's wonderful workshop, My Second Brain (you find similar content on Youtube) — the idea that you have your own wiki which Claude Code builds from a folder with raw files. The idea landed, and I got to work creating brains one at a time.

First I had just the one that helps me with organizing my tool knowledge, mostly in the unfathomable depths of ABAP documentation. Realizing that I cannot have one large brain for all my needs, I started to create other ones, to avoid dilution of knowledge. I created another one for the new field of code-based agents, and one for personal development which became a pretty cool career advisor as we are currently preparing our Q3 SAP talks. And another one for API and Business Object documentation.

Keeping them separate was intentional. A single mega-brain collapses under its own weight, mixing goal-setting advice with ABAP transaction quirks.

The trade-off: Claude only knew about the individual ones when you worked in the corresponding project folder. Sit inside one brain and drift into a topic that belongs to another, and Claude has no way of knowing the other brain even exists. The answer comes from raw model knowledge — plausible, generic, and sometimes wrong.

The fix: a coordinating brain

The solution turned out to be smaller than expected. Not a fifth brain, but a compact registry — one table in the global CLAUDE.md that every Claude session loads automatically. Each row names a brain, its path, its scope, and the topics that trigger a consult. Two rules follow: read the matching brain's index before answering, and reconcile across brains when a topic straddles two of them.

That's it. No new folders, no orchestration layer, no third-party tooling. Just a lookup table that lives where Claude already looks.

The octopus metaphor writes itself. The four brains are the arms — specialized, autonomous, each holding its own hard-won knowledge. The registry is the central brain, unimpressive on paper, doing the one thing central brains do well: routing signals to the arm that already knows the answer.

 

Try it yourself

If you have two or more brains and want the same routing setup, drop the following into a Claude session and let it do the discovery and wiring for you.

Note: Your brain folders might not be called `mybrain<Name>` — adapt the naming in the prompt below to match your own convention.

Here's the prompt:

I have multiple "second brain" folders on my machine — each is a folder named `mybrain<Something>` following the LLM-Wiki pattern, with `raw/`, `wiki/`, and a `CLAUDE.md` at the root. Right now Claude only sees whichever brain I happen to be sitting in, so knowledge in the other brains stays invisible for the session. Fix that. Steps:

1. **Find all my brains.** Search my user home directory and its subfolders (including any OneDrive folders) for directories matching `mybrain*` that contain a `CLAUDE.md` at their root. Skip `.zip` archives, empty folders, and staging folders that lack the full brain structure.

2. **Read each brain's `CLAUDE.md`** to pull out its purpose and scope. Do not modify anything inside the brain folders.

3. **Locate my global Claude config** at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (Windows: `C:\Users\<me>\.claude\CLAUDE.md`). If it does not exist, stop and tell me before creating anything.

4. **Prepare a new section** titled `## Personal Knowledge Bases (mybrain* wikis)` containing:
- A one-line introduction to the routing pattern.
- A markdown table with one row per brain, columns: **Brain**, **Path**, **Scope**, **Consult when**. Derive "Consult when" from each brain's own scope, pulling out two or three concrete topic keywords.
- A short workflow: read the matching brain's `wiki/index.md` first, cite pages, reconcile across brains when a topic spans two of them, fall back to model knowledge only when no brain covers the topic.
- A rule that each brain's `raw/` folder is read-only.

5. **Show me the preview** and ask me to confirm before writing the section into the global CLAUDE.md. Preserve everything already in that file — append or splice in cleanly, do not overwrite unrelated sections.

6. **After writing**, tell me how to verify: open a new Claude session, ask a question that clearly belongs to one specific brain from any folder on disk, and confirm Claude routes to that brain's wiki before answering.

Do not create any new brain folders. Do not modify anything inside the existing brains. Only edit the global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`.

Closing thoughts

That's the whole pattern: four arms of specialized knowledge, one small table in the middle, no orchestration layer. Total wiring cost was one section in a markdown file, and Claude now routes across brains without me having to remember which folder I'm sitting in.

If you're running multiple wikis and want the same routing, the prompt above will set it up for you. And if you've built a different way of stitching separate knowledge bases together, drop it in the comments — I'd like to see what other shapes this pattern can take.

entry.title: Re: Problem with Product SOA message not triggered during...
entry.content:
Hi Deepali,

Since you've already debugged CL_MDC_MODEL_MAT=>REPLICATE_MATERIAL, it looks like the issue occurs before the DRF API is called. If MATNR is blank in MARA_SRC_STAT, <ls_mat_drf_keys>-bo_keys will remain initial, so CL_DRF_MEMBERSHIP_API=>SEND_BO_INSTANCE_MULTI won't be executed.

I would suggest checking where LT_MARA_SRC is populated and whether MATNR is already blank at that stage. If it is, the problem is likely in the material selection or key preparation rather than in the DRF framework itself.

It would also be worth verifying the replication model and filters to make sure the material is included for replication.

Could you let us know whether MATNR is already blank before entering REPLICATE_MATERIAL, or does it become blank inside the method?

entry.title: Re: SQL Anywhere 17: How can DATABASE SIZE be used with a...
entry.content: I am not aware of any mechanism to size the database created during a dbunload operation.  Although, I believe that a database created by dbunload does get sized based on the source database - but it is conservative and would not reflect that actual size of the end database.  It also would not account for any future growth which I think the formula posted is intended to account for.

entry.title: Ever wondered how SAP EWM decides which RF queue a Warehouse Task lands in?
entry.content:
Queue Determination is the rule-based logic that routes every Warehouse Task to the right queue — matching Warehouse Number, Process Type, Activity Area, Storage Type, and Resource Group against a customizing table. Get this configuration wrong, and tasks either sit in the wrong queue or don't get picked up at all.
I put together a visual breakdown covering: → What Queue Determination actually is → The factors the system reads on a Warehouse Task → The resulting queue types and where they route → The 3-part structure of a determination rule → Key terms + where it's configured in the IMG → The end-to-end flow, from task creation to RF execution
Swipe through and let me know — what's the trickiest queue determination issue you've hit in a live project?
#SAPEWM #SAP #ExtendedWarehouseManagement #WarehouseManagement #SupplyChain #Logistics #SAPS4HANA #WMS #QueueDetermination #WarehouseTasks #SAPConsultant #DigitalSupplyChain #WarehouseAutomation

entry.title: Transfering document based test cases from Solution Manager 7.2 to Cloud ALM
entry.content:
The challenge


Selective data transfer (SDT) allows to migrate test cases in "Test Steps" format from Solution Manager 7.2 to CLoud ALM.
However, if your test cases in Solution Manager happen to be document based (for example word/excel) SDT will only convert them to "test documents" in Cloud ALM, but they will not be available as actual test cases. 
This is an issue because all the test management functionality in Cloud ALM only works with native Cloud ALM test cases, but not with documents. For example only a native Cloud ALM test case an be added to a test plan and executed.  This article outlines an approach how to still bring document based test cases to CALM.


Solution part 1 of 3: Downloading test documents from Solution Manager 7.2

First of all:
If you happen to have your test case documents available outside of solution manager (on a network drive or sharepoint), you are in luck, because you can skip this step.
In case you don't, follow the steps below:

  1. In Solution Manager 7.2, go to Solution Documentation. Be sure to have the correct solution and branch selected and navigate to the (hierarchically lowest)  folder containing all test documents to be downloaded. Probably your test documents will be spread out across several scenarios and processes, so that folder will likely be " Business processes".

  2. Switch from "Browser" view to "List" view. While browser view only displays documents assigned to the current folder, list view will include documents assigned to all subfolders - so we don't have to navigate to and download each folder separately.


  3. In List view, filter the displayed documents by type to display only type "Test document".  


  4. Click the top check box to select all displayed documents. Next, right click one of the selected documents and select from the context menu "Document download (only originals)".  This will attempt to download a .sap file to your frontend. 
      

  5. Opening the file will prompt for a download directory on your local computer. Once selected you may be prompted an additional 2 times for permissions to write to that folder and to execute an ABAP report on Solution Manager. Provided these permissions are given, the selected test documents should be downloaded to the selected folder:  

Solution part 2 of 3: Transforming test documents to Cloud ALM test cases using a coding agent

The complexity of this part will vary depending on how uniform the test documents are in terms of file type and formatting (the more variety, the more complex the task / the  prompt will be). In this example we use excel test cases with a rather uniform formatting and employ claude code as a tool to 

  • read all downloaded excel test documents from an "input" folder
  • convert them to the Cloud ALM upload excel format (specified by example Cloud ALM test cases in a "template" folder) with a specific column mapping.
  • save them (the CLoud ALM test cases)  all in 1 file (in the "output" folder) - so we can upload all test cases to Cloud ALM in one go.

Probably other tools like windsurf or codex will work just as well. What's important is their ability to access and make changes to the local file system.  For this example the below prompt was used:

---
In the current folder you will find:
- a folder "input", containing the excel test cases to be converted. 1 excel file is 1 test case.
- a folder "template" containing the format to convert into. This format supports multiple test cases in 1 excel file.

The target format has a 3-tier hierarchy: a test Case contains one or more actions.
An Action consists of 1 or more activities. Each activity has a title, an instruction and an expected result.
There may be more columns in the target format.

Convert all test cases in "input" to the format found in "template" and put them all into the same excel file. Save that file (the converted test cases) to the "output" folder as (named like a timestamp): "YYYYMMDD_hhmm.xlsx".

Column mapping (template column <-- input column):
Test Case Name <-- Sequence
Activity Title <-- Description
Action Title <-- No. Sequence, Name (if Name is empty, use Description instead)
Action Instruction <-- Node Text
Action Expected Result <-- Document No
Test Case Status = "Prepared" (fixed, for each test case)

If Action Title, Action Instruction or Action Expected Result are empty, enter "-".
Work autonomously and do not ask for any confirmations or permissions.
---

 

Before continuing:
Being the responsible "humans in the loop", that we are, we of course check whether what the AI created still resembles a test case and that no relevant information has been lost. I recommend to at least check one or two examples for each file type and document format.

 

Solution part 3 of 3: Uploading converted test cases to Cloud ALM

Before upload the test cases to Cloud ALM, it's recommended to create a single purpose scope to use for the upload, especially if you already have test cases in Cloud ALM. Why that? We will specify that scope when uploading (at least for the first time), so all uploaded test cases get assigned to that scope. If  we find that something wrong with the test cases in Cloud ALM post upload, we can simply select all uploaded test cases by that scope, delete them and start another attempt.
A scope can be created in Cloud ALM under Implementation --> Projects and Setup --> <Your project> --> Scopes:

Just create the simplest version of a scope with just a title - don't select any solution processes.
The test case upload can now be performed from Cloud ALM's "Test Preparation" app, and is initiated through the upload button:

entry.title: Designing Experiential Learning in SAP S/4HANA Classrooms
entry.content:
How can we teach SAP S/4HANA to management students who enter the classroom with widely different levels of technical knowledge and professional experience?

This question has guided the development of my postgraduate elective, Introduction to SAP S/4HANA ERP. I recently had the opportunity to share this teaching approach at the SAP Academic Community Conference APAC 2026.

The course is designed as a hands-on, continuous-assessment-based elective for MBA and integrated programme students. Across all the cohorts since inception, the course has consistently reached its maximum capacity of students - hinting at the interest in SAP. The participants range from students with no prior work experience to practicing professionals, including a small number who already have exposure to SAP.

Moving beyond transaction codes

A central objective of the course is to help students understand SAP not simply as a software interface, but as a platform connecting business processes, organizational functions, data and managerial decisions.

The course therefore begins with the business relevance of enterprise resource planning. Students first explore why organizations require integrated systems before progressing to SAP navigation, master data, transaction codes and table-level linkages.

Hands-on exercises using the Global Bike/GBI environment help students connect individual system actions with broader end-to-end business processes. Rather than memorizing a sequence of transactions, students are encouraged to ask

  • What business event does this transaction represent?
  • Which organizational function is responsible for it?
  • What data are created or updated?
  • How does the action affect subsequent stages of the process?

This approach helps students recognize the relationship between front-end system activities, underlying data structures and cross-functional business flows.

Combining systems, cases and industry perspectives

Experiential learning is supported through a combination of system exercises, case analysis, analytics tools and industry interaction.

Students engage with SAP Business Builder and SAP Analytics Cloud to examine business scenarios and analytical decision-making. Selected cases are incorporated into time-gated assessments, enabling students to apply their understanding under realistic constraint

Case studies and research articles from peer-reviewed publications are also used to connect classroom activities with wider questions involving digital transformation, process integration, analytics and enterprise strategy.

Industry immersion is another important component. Guest sessions with practitioners and participation in SAP events allow students to understand how classroom concepts translate into consulting, functional, technical and managerial roles. Students are also encouraged to participate in external experiential opportunities such as ERPsim competitions

Designing assessment as a learning journey

The assessment structure follows the progression of the course.

Students begin with introductory quizzes that evaluate their understanding of ERP concepts and business use cases. They then move to guided system exercises, in-class assessments, advanced business-flow activities and tool-based analytical tasks.

Group case analyses require students to identify a business problem and propose an appropriate ERP-enabled solution. The emphasis is not solely on arriving at a technically correct answer, but on explaining the business rationale behind the recommendation.

Active participation, experimentation and reflection are encouraged throughout the course. At its conclusion, participants receive a course-completion certificate jointly offered by the institution and SAP.

Integrating AI judiciously

Generative AI is incorporated carefully rather than treated as either a prohibited shortcut or an automatic solution.

Students may use GenAI to support activities such as preliminary case exploration, structuring business problems and evaluating possible solution alternatives. However, they remain responsible for validating outputs, connecting recommendations with business processes and demonstrating their own understanding.

The course also introduces students to the growing role of AI within enterprise applications, including the incorporation of AI agents and capabilities such as Joule. This creates an opportunity to discuss not only what AI can perform, but how AI-generated recommendations should be evaluated within an organizational context.

From the classroom to professional pathways

The intended outcome is not to produce SAP specialists within a single introductory elective. Instead, the course provides a structured starting point from which students can pursue SAP Learning Hub resources, formal certifications and module-specific learning pathways.

More broadly, SAP-based experiential learning strengthens students’ understanding of business processes across the management curriculum. It can help them explore professional directions in ERP consulting, functional and technical roles, business analytics, and application development.

My key learning from teaching this course is that SAP education becomes most meaningful when students can connect four elements:

business context, system interaction, data and decision-making.

When these elements are taught together, students move beyond learning how to complete a transaction. They begin to understand why the transaction exists, how it connects to the organization and how enterprise systems support decisions from the classroom to the boardroom.

The presentation also led to valuable interactions during the Q&A session. Faculty members shared concerns and practical challenges from their own classrooms, including managing students with varied levels of prior knowledge, balancing conceptual understanding with hands-on system exposure, and designing an effective course-delivery sequence within limited teaching time. These conversations reinforced the value of exchanging classroom experiences and adapting SAP teaching practices to different institutional and learner contexts.

I look forward to continuing these conversations and learning how other educators design experiential SAP activities for cohorts with diverse academic, technical and professional backgrounds. Kudos to the organizers for setting up this platform for the discussion!

entry.title: Re: Product Category in Purchase Contract defaults to Mat...
entry.content:
Dear @Subodh_Chordiya,

No. The Product Type Group cannot be left blank to make one purchase contract item usable for both materials and services

The field defines whether the individual contract item is:

  • 1 Material
  • 2 Service

A single purchase contract can cover both materials and services but they must be created as separate contract items with the appropriate Product Type Group. A blank value does not function as a wildcard for both categories

For service contract items, SAP recommends using a service product with material type SERV. This is particularly relevant when purchase orders are created with reference to the contract, because service contract items without a product number may default back to Product Type Group 1 – Material in the purchase order. In that case the Product Type Group can be set manually during purchase order creation (apps Manage Purchase Orders or Create Purchase Order – Advanced / ME21N)

Summary:

A single purchase contract can cover both types of procurement but each contract item must be classified separately as Material or Service

IMPORTANT: SAP has noted that copying the Product Type Group from a purchase contract is planned as a feature request for a future release without a committed release date

More details below:

SAP Help – Manage Purchase Contracts

SAP KBA 2917410 – Product Type Group and Service Performer Not Adopted from Service Purchase Contract

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud Framework Update — JDK 21: Top 3 Issues and How to Resolve Them
entry.content:
Upgrading to JDK 21 is a significant step in your SAP Commerce Cloud journey. While the migration brings important changes and security improvements, it also introduces a set of challenges that many customers encounter, particularly during and after their first production deployment.

This post covers the three most common issues we see, what causes them, and exactly what to do to resolve them.

1. Deployment Gets Stuck at the "Migrate Data" Step

KBA 3697105 — Deployment with Migrate Data Stuck during Upgrade to JDK 21

What you see: Your deployment has been running for several hours with no visible progress. No explicit errors appear in the logs. In the platform-update pod logs, you see repeated messages from SessionCtxValueJDBCCleaner, and almost no new log entries are being printed. Checking dynatrace methods hotspots from the migration pod shows the main thread stuck in LegacyOAuthCronJobsCleanUp.removeLegacyOAuthCleaningCronJobs.

Why this is the most common cause: This is the single most frequently reported issue during a JDK 21 upgrade. The upgrade to JDK 21 and Spring Framework 6.2 introduces a new data migration step called removeLegacyOAuthCleaningCronJobs, which removes all entries linked to the legacy OAuth cleanup cron jobs (cleanupOAuthAccessTokenPerformable, cleanupOAuthRefreshTokenPerformable, cleanupOAuthAuthorizationCodePerformable). In production environments where these log files and the CronJobHistory table have not been regularly maintained, this cleanup can accumulate a very large number of records, causing the migration to slow down significantly and eventually time out. Setting jdbc.clean.sessionContextValues to false in hcs_admin does not bypass this step.

How to resolve it:

Before your next deployment attempt — clean up the data:

Option A (Recommended) — Set up a maintenance CronJob:

Configure a dedicated CronJob using cleanUpLogsJobPerformable with a retention policy. Example ImpEx:

$time = 1209600
INSERT_UPDATE FlexibleSearchRetentionRule;code[unique=true];searchQuery;retentionTimeSeconds;actionReference;
;logFilesRule;"SELECT {p:pk},{p:itemtype} FROM {LogFile AS p} WHERE {p:modifiedTime} < ?CALC_RETIREMENT_TIME";$time;basicRemoveCleanupAction;

INSERT_UPDATE RetentionJob;code[unique=true];retentionRule(code);batchSize
;logFilesCleanupJob;logFilesRule;10000

INSERT_UPDATE CronJob;code[unique=true];job(code);sessionLanguage(isoCode)[default=en]
;logFilesCleanupCronJob;logFilesCleanupJob;

INSERT_UPDATE Trigger;cronJob(code)[unique=true];cronExpression
;logFilesCleanupCronJob;0 0/2 * * * ?

Once the number of log files is reduced, the migrate data step will complete successfully.

Option B — Direct cleanup via HAC:

  1. Find the PKs of the three legacy OAuth cron jobs via FlexibleSearch in HAC:
select {pk} from {cronjob} where {code} in
('cleanOAuthAccessTokenCronJob','cleanOAuthRefreshTokenCronJob','cleanOAuthAuthorizationCodeCronJob')
  1. Check and delete their associated log files in the SQL Query tab (commit mode):
delete from medias where OwnerPkString in ('<pk1>','<pk2>','<pk3>')
  1. Delete the cron jobs themselves:
delete from cronjobs where p_code in
('cleanOAuthAccessTokenCronJob','cleanOAuthRefreshTokenCronJob','cleanOAuthAuthorizationCodeCronJob')
  1. Also check and clear the CronJobHistory table for these jobs if records exist.

Important: Do not roll back while the database is still showing active work. A long deployment is not the same as a failed deployment.

2. Login Fails with 403 Forbidden After the Upgrade — CSRF Check Failed

KBA 3737709 — 403 Forbidden with CSRF check failed for JS Storefront Custom Login Page after upgrade to JDK 21

What you see: After a successful deployment, some users cannot access the custom composable storefront login page, specifically when using Safari, Chrome in incognito mode, or Microsoft Edge. The request returns an HTTP 403 Forbidden error and there is no redirect back to the login page. Comparing the HTTP headers between a working and a failing session shows key difference: the failing request has no JSESSIONID or ROUTE cookie, and sec-fetch-storage-access is reported as none instead of active.

Why this is the most common cause: This is one of the most disruptive post-upgrade surprises because it passes completely undetected in lower environments, which typically use standard out-of-the-box domains. Starting with SAP Commerce Cloud 2211-jdk21.1, the Authorization Code Flow requires the custom login page and the API/authorization server to be hosted on the same domain or subdomain. When they are on separate domains, modern browsers treat the session cookie (JSESSIONID) as a third-party cookie and block it from being sent in cross-site requests. Without the session cookie, the authorization server cannot identify the session, and the CSRF check fails.

How to resolve it:

The fix is to configure a custom domain for your API service so that the JS Storefront login page and the authorization server share the same domain or a compatible same-site domain.

  1. Identify your current JS Storefront custom domain (e.g. storefront.your-domain.com)
  2. Create an SSL certificate for your API domain via the Cloud Portal
  3. Create an endpoint for the API service with a matching custom domain (e.g. api.your-domain.com) and assign the certificate
  4. Check the load balancer IP under Cloud Portal > Endpoints > API > Web proxy, and update your DNS settings accordingly
  5. Update your JS Storefront configuration to point the OCC baseUrl to the new API domain:
<meta name="occ-backend-base-url" content="https://api.your-domain.com" />
  1. Verify that both domains fall under the same registrable domain (eTLD+1) to satisfy browser SameSite cookie policy
  2. Re-test the login flow on Safari, Chrome incognito, and Edge to confirm that sec-fetch-storage-access now shows as active

Note: This issue only appears with browsers that enforce strict third-party cookie policies. Testing exclusively in Chrome (non-incognito) may give a false pass.

3. Existing Users Cannot Log In After the Upgrade — Password Hashing

KBA 3705251 — Managing the Removal of Deprecated Password Hashing Algorithms During Upgrade to Versions Above 2211.47/2211-jdk21.5

What you see: The deployment completes without errors, but existing users — storefront customers and Backoffice/admin users — cannot log in. Newly created accounts work fine. The password reset flow also fails because it tries to read a hash stored in a deprecated format that no longer exists.

Why this is the most common cause: This issue is almost invisible in lower environments because test accounts are freshly created and already use the current hashing algorithm. As of 2211.47 / 2211-jdk21.5, SAP Commerce removed deprecated password hashing encoders entirely. This was introduced as part of the Improved Security of Password Handling feature rolled out in version 2211.28. If the password.encoding.auto.update.enabled property was not set to true before the upgrade, existing users whose passwords were stored with the old algorithm will not be able to log in after the upgrade.

Important version note: It is recommended to skip 2211.47 / 2211-jdk21.5 entirely due to a known password reset issue in that specific release, and upgrade directly to 2211.48 / 2211-jdk21.6.

How to resolve it:

Before your upgrade (most important step):

Enable automatic password rehashing as early as possible to give users a grace period to log in and have their passwords silently rehashed:

password.encoding.auto.update.enabled=true

Verify the rehashing status in HAC using:

select p_name, p_uid, p_passwordencoding from users where {{your_condition}};

If the passwordencoding column shows argon2, the password has been successfully rehashed.

It is also recommended to set:

toggle.disallowTaeEncryptionFallback.enabled=true

to prevent potential security risks from plaintext fallback during system initialization.

After your upgrade — for users who did not log in before the update:

  • Storefront (customer) users — can reset their own password using the standard "Forgot password" flow
  • Backoffice / backend (employee) users — passwords must be manually reset by an administrator; no self-service option is available
  • Admin users — can use the Generate New Password function in the Cloud Portal via the Customer System Administrator (CSA) role

Key Takeaway

All three of these issues share one important characteristic: they are difficult or impossible to catch in staging because they depend on production data volumes, real legacy user accounts, and production-only infrastructure like custom domains and CDN configurations.

Before triggering your production deployment, make sure you have:

  • ✅Cleaned up CronJobHistory and OAuth cron job log files ahead of time
  • ✅Set password.encoding.auto.update.enabled=true well before your upgrade window
  • ✅Verified your storefront and API domains are aligned on the same registrable domain
  • ✅Tested the login flow with real legacy user accounts, not freshly created ones
  • ✅Estimated your production deployment window based on actual data volumes, not staging timings

For the full resolution steps, always refer to the linked KBAs directly in SAP for Me — or ask the Joule AI assistant to guide you to the right article based on your specific error.

entry.title: SAP APAC 2026 Research Seminar: From Clicks to Recommendations
entry.content:
From Clicks to Recommendations: Rethinking Clickbait in Algorithmically Curated Social Media

At the SAP Academic Community Conference APAC 2026, I presented our research titled From Clicks to Recommendations: The Implications of Clickbait in Algorithmically Curated Social Media.

The study begins with a simple concern: social media visibility is increasingly determined by recommendation systems, while clickbait has expanded beyond sensational headlines. It now includes thumbnails, preview frames, captions and emotionally charged visual cues designed to capture attention.

These cues encourage users to click, watch, comment and share. Recommendation systems may then interpret these behaviours as indicators of relevance or quality. However, high engagement does not always mean that users received the value promised by the content packaging.

Our research therefore considers clickbait as a form of multimodal signal design within algorithmically curated platforms. The key concern is the possible mismatch between the attention cue and the content ultimately delivered.

The study conceptually examines how such mismatches may influence the engagement signals used by recommendation systems. It also considers the dual role of artificial intelligence: AI may improve platforms’ ability to identify inconsistencies between titles, thumbnails and content, while generative AI may also enable increasingly personalised, synthetic and emotionally optimized attention cues.

The presentation highlighted the need to look beyond clicks and viewing time when evaluating content quality. Platforms may need to combine behavioural engagement with richer indicators of user satisfaction, trust and title-thumbnail-content congruence.

The Q&A session generated valuable discussion around the distinction between legitimate attention-grabbing communication and deceptive content packaging. Participants also raised questions about platform responsibility, the measurement of user satisfaction and the influence of generative AI on future content and recommendation practices.

These interactions highlighted the value of connecting research across marketing, information systems, analytics, responsible AI and platform governance. We look forward to developing the study further and continuing the conversation around responsible digital engagement and AI-mediated recommendation.

entry.title: Re: Lockbox Processing in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.
entry.content:
Dear @S0027020516,

The best official starting point for Public Cloud is the SAP Best Practices scope item Lockbox (1JT). In SAP Signavio Process Navigator, select SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition the current release and the United States country version. The scope-item package is the primary source for the end-to-end process flow, setup instructions, test procedure, required roles and available implementation accelerators

For manual file testing, BAI2 is the standard lockbox format explicitly supported by the Manage Incoming Payment Files (F1680) app. SAP Help also provides a detailed lockbox record-format description with a sample file that can be used as the basis for testing

A useful minimum test pack should include:

  • Customer and invoice identified payment is automatically posted and cleared
  • Customer identified, but invoice not identified payment is posted on account or sent for reprocessing, depending on the processing-rule configuration
  • Neither customer nor invoice identified the item remains incomplete and must be handled in Reprocess Lockbox Items
  • Partial payment
  • One payment covering multiple invoices
  • Overpayment or payment difference
  • Invalid bank account, routing number, customer MICR information, or invoice reference
  • Duplicate-file or duplicate-payment scenario
  • Processing-rule scenarios configured in Manage Processing Rules For Lockbox (F8134)

Lockbox files can be imported through:

  • SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity
  • Lockbox - Post API
  • Manage Incoming Payment Files (F1680)

The inbound API is API_LOCKBOXPOST_IN, uses OData V2, belongs to communication scenario SAP_COM_0759 Finance Accounts Receivable Lockbox Integration and is designated by SAP as US-specific

Items that cannot be posted and cleared automatically can be reviewed and completed in Reprocess Lockbox Items (F3398). Processing rules can be created and automated in Manage Processing Rules For Lockbox (F8134)

More details below:

SAP Signavio Process Navigator – Lockbox (1JT)

SAP Help – Overview on Automatic Lockbox Processing

SAP Help – Format Description for Lockbox Batches

SAP Help – Manage Incoming Payment Files

SAP Help – How to Import Payment Files

SAP Help – Manage Processing Rules – For Lockbox

SAP Help – Reprocess Lockbox Items

SAP Help – Lockbox – Post API

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP Business Network for Logistics - Dock Appointment Scheduling: Loading Point Restriction
entry.content:
Hi @siva93, 

The attributes are defined at the Loading Point (LP) level and apply to all appointments booked for that LP. There is no difference between appointments booked by onboarded carriers and guest carriers, as the same attribute validation rules apply to both.

During appointment scheduling, Loading Points are filtered from the list of available options if they do not match all required shipment attributes. This ensures that only compliant Loading Points are presented for booking.

Regards, Marco

entry.title: Re: SAP DM Release order using REO to create a custom SFC...
entry.content:
Hi @aldreensansone,

Business Services are not triggered from REO Release, only for API release, check Order Release, for:

In the Manage Automatic Triggers app, you can choose between two business services to release orders:

  • Release Order (Deprecated)

    Use this business service if you want to trigger the service interception (Before Service Execution, Error During Service Execution, After Service Execution) from the user interface of the Manage Orders app.

  • Release Order

    Use this business service if you want to trigger the service interception (Before Service Execution, Error During Service Execution, After Service Execution) using the Order API (order/v2/orders/release).

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: Discover the 18 AI-Assisted Procedures in the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series that introduces AI in SAP Activate. For more information, visit the main blog "SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation."

With the 2608 release, the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap now includes 18 AI-assisted procedures designed to help implementation teams accelerate common project activities.

These procedures are available throughout the implementation lifecycle, supporting activities in the Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy phases.

📷 Image 1 – AI-assisted procedures distributed across the roadmapFinding AI-Assisted Procedures

The easiest way to locate AI-assisted procedures in the roadmap is by using the AI-Assisted Procedure tag to browse all AI-enabled tasks.

📷 Image 2 – Searching for AI-Assisted Procedure in the Road Map Viewer

What to Expect

Each AI-assisted procedure provides guidance for a specific implementation activity. Depending on the task, you'll find:

  • Recommended prompts
  • Instructions for using SAP Joule for Consultants
  • Guidance for generating implementation artifacts
  • Tips for reviewing and refining AI-generated content

The goal is not to replace consultant expertise, but to help teams get started more quickly and consistently.

📷 Image 3 – Example of an AI-assisted procedure

Prompt Library

Many AI-assisted procedures include a Prompt Library with ready-to-use prompts that can be copied, adapted to your project context, and executed using SAP Joule for Consultants.

These prompts provide a solid starting point and help reduce the effort required to create common project deliverables.

📷 Image 4 – Prompt Library within an AI-assisted procedure

Start Exploring

The addition of 18 AI-assisted procedures marks another step in bringing AI directly into the SAP Activate methodology. Whether you're planning workshops, documenting requirements, or preparing for deployment, these procedures help you identify where AI can accelerate your work while keeping SAP Activate at the center of your implementation approach.

Ready to explore them? Open the SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private – New Implementation roadmap in the SAP Road Map Viewer and search for "AI-Assisted Procedure" to discover all available AI-enabled tasks.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud - New Search and Replace actions in filter token
entry.content:
Introduction

When filtering in SAP Analytics Cloud, you're usually doing one of two things: quickly finding a set of members and applying them immediately or refining your selection step by step before committing. The redesigned search experience in the Q3 2026 release is built around both - using the new Replace and Update actions.

New Search and Replace: Get to Your Filter Selection Faster

There is no need to manually deselecting "All" before you start. When you enter a search term, the previous selection is no longer in the way.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

During an active search, the “Apply Selection” button discards the previous selected members and replace them with the current search result directly.

For more complex selections, run multiple consecutive searches to build up your final list before committing:

  • Replace starts fresh: the current selection is completely discarded and replaced with the search result. Members that were previously selected but are not in the search results will be removed.
  • Update adds to what you have: Search results are merged into the existing selection. Previously selected members are kept, and any search results are added on top. Any deselections made during the search are also applied.
  • “Apply Selection" commits the draft and closes the search.

 

 

Whether you are filtering with a single search or composing a list across multiple searches: all search results are pre-selected. Users can confirm immediately or refine from there, without having to manually select each member first.

Availability

This feature is planned for SAP Analytics Cloud Q3.2026 QRC and is expected to be available across all major filter contexts:

  • Story Filter
  • Page Filter
  • Widget Filter

entry.title: Re: SEN Cloud Producer errors of "Bad allocation" or "Win...
entry.content:
Okay, I figured it out. We had more than 15,000 objects in the Workspace, which traditionally has not been an issue because there is no published limit to the number of objects in the Workspace, and I have had 35,000 objects in an On-Premise SEN Workspace n the past. But at least a third of that was Unpublished content that we were just keeping because it was content a past contractor had built, and the customer had paid for it, so we didn't want to Delete it. So I Exported that Unpublished content to a .dkp, then set up a new Workspace and Imported the .dkp into it. (The Manager Export/Import functions have never worked in any SEN system I have used, and they didn't work when I tried them this time. I don't care - Producer is more effective anyway.) When I tried to Finish Editing and Sync in Producer after the .dkp was Imported to the new Workspace, I got the same errors there in the new Workspace. So I knew the content was the problem. There was nothing special about it, just the usual Books, Book Pages, Simulations, Quizzes and Text Units. But something was corrupted there. 

Once I was sure the .dkp was Imported to the new Workspace, I deleted that content from the originating Workspace. At that point, the Producer errors stopped. That's double proof that the deleted content was the problem. The new Workspace is just storage, so I don't care if the errors occur there - we're not using it for anything.

SAP Support also replied later and said they recommend keeping a Workspace <10,000 objects, because they have seen problems occur when that number is exceeded. We are back down to ~9,500 objects now in the original Workspace, so we will reach 10,000 in next year or so. We'll get to test whether that "limit" is really a limit.

entry.title: Is SAP BTP required between SAP Commerce(OCC APIS) and React JS(Hosted in AWS)?
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

We are planning to migrate our existing storefront to a headless architecture, with React as the frontend and SAP Commerce as the backend. For the OCC APIs that SAP Commerce will expose, do we need SAP BTP as middleware, or can the React application hosted on AWS consume the OCC APIs directly? Please advise.

entry.title: Re: Is SAP BTP required between SAP Commerce(OCC APIS) an...
entry.content:
SAP BTP is not required as middleware for your React application on AWS to consume SAP Commerce OCC APIs. The OCC (Omni Commerce Connect) REST API is technology-agnostic and framework-agnostic, meaning a React frontend hosted on any cloud provider can consume it directly.

SAP BTP as middleware is more relevant in scenarios where: 

  • You are connecting to an SAP S/4HANA on-premise backend (not SAP Commerce Cloud) and need the Cloud Connector to avoid opening firewall ports.
  • You need integration orchestration between SAP Commerce and other SAP backend systems (e.g., S/4HANA) — in which case SAP Cloud Integration (part of BTP) is used as the middleware layer.

entry.title: Re: Celebrate Your SAP Academic Community Conference experience: Earn Your Exclusive Conference Badg
entry.content:
Hello! I recently had the opportunity to attend the SAP Academic Community Conference North America (NA) 2026 as both an attendee and a speaker. It's one of the highlights of my summer each year, and I always leave energized with new ideas, new connections, and a renewed excitement for the upcoming academic year.

Speaker Session: Fireside Chat: Building Bridges with ASUG

The SAP Academic Community Conference is one of my favorite events because it brings together faculty, SAP leaders, and industry partners who are all committed to advancing SAP education. Every session, workshop, and conversation offered new ideas about AI, the future of enterprise education, and innovative ways to prepare students for the careers ahead.

It was a pleasure to participate in the Fireside Chat: Building Bridges with ASUG, where we discussed how engagement with ASUG creates meaningful opportunities for faculty, strengthens connections with industry, and helps better prepare students for successful careers in the SAP ecosystem.

One of the biggest takeaways for me was the opportunity to collaborate with colleagues from across North America. Some of the best ideas came from conversations between sessions, sharing classroom experiences, discussing challenges, and learning from one another. Those connections are what make this community so valuable.

A special thank you to SAP, the SAP University Alliances team, and the SAP Academic Community North America Board for the tremendous amount of work that goes into planning and delivering this conference each year. It is no small task to create an event that is both engaging and meaningful, and your dedication to supporting faculty and strengthening our academic community does not go unnoticed.

I’m already looking forward to next year's conference and continuing the conversations that started in Irvine.

entry.title: Re: Business Data Cloud & Datasphere: Are the Needs of Mid-Sized Companies Being Adequately Cons
entry.content: Hi Niels, Thanks for sharing this — it resonates a lot with what we're seeing across several client engagements as well. We regularly work on SAP Datasphere / BDC pricing and architecture comparisons for mid-sized organizations, and the Capacity Unit sizing tied to Object Store access comes up as a recurring pain point. The pattern you describe — a minimum CU threshold that's clearly calibrated for enterprise-scale productive systems being non-negotiable even for a lightweight test/dev instance — is something we've run into repeatedly. It effectively forces customers into your Option 1 (oversized purchase) even when the actual use case (piloting Data Products, testing AI scenarios, small-scale historization) would fit comfortably into a fraction of that capacity. What compounds this for mid-market customers specifically is that the CU model doesn't really scale down gracefully — there's a floor that doesn't track with actual usage/storage needs, which makes PoC-style adoption economically awkward exactly at the stage where you'd want low commitment to justify further investment. It would be great to see SAP introduce a genuine "sandbox" tier for Object Store access — decoupled from the full CU floor — specifically for non-productive systems. Given how central Data Products and BDC are to SAP's current AI narrative, keeping the entry cost of experimentation reasonable for mid-sized companies seems like it should be a priority, not an afterthought. Following this thread with interest — curious whether others have found leverage points with their account teams on this.

You obviously have to connect Entra:  https://help.sap.com/docs/identity-authentication/identity-authentication/corporate-identity-providers

Then you obviously need to connect SAP Applications, BWZ, Signavio, others. And for each application you forward the relevant attributes, where obviously they need to have some kind of identifier, but let's say part of the Entra groups have BWZ or SIG in them, you use regex to filter out what groups you want to forward

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/user-attributes?locale=en-US

I highly recommend CIS, it's very modular, free and very good at what it does - ensuring you can add the SAP touch to work with all your systems. I'm fairly sure it's even becoming more and more an obligation, for example for certain SAP Cloud products or for the use of Joule. Read the SAP CIO identity lifecycle guide to get a better understanding of how this all works together. And mostly just works.

We went from 10 SAP-systems and 20 Cloud systems that were mostly just username+password to full SSO and nearly completely automatically provisioned users, linked to our IAM-tooling in a little over a year. I did most of that on my own - while doing other projects and sometimes having to wait weeks for certain infra or certificate teams.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
1. srv/game-service.js

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
    async init() {

        const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities;

        // ── startTurn
        // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
        // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
        // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
        this.on("startTurn", "Players", async (req) => {
            // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
            // console.log("startTurn action triggered")
        })

        // ── rollDice
        // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
        // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
        this.on("rollDice", "Players", async (req) => {
            // console.log("rollDice action triggered")
            const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
            if (!player) return req.error(400, "Player not found");

            const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
            let newPosition = player.position + roll;

            // Validate that the newPosition doesn't go beyond 100 (the BoardSquares boundry)
            if (newPosition > 100) {
                // await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ID: player.ID})
                const needed = 100 - player.position
                return req.error(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move`)
            }

            // Resolve snake or ladded on the new square (newPosition)
            const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition });
            let finalPosition = newPosition
            let event = null

            if (square?.ladderTo) {
                finalPosition = square.ladderTo
                event = { type: "ladder", "from": newPosition, "to": finalPosition }
            } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
                finalPosition = square.snakeTo;
                event = square.isDoubleHead
                    ? { type: "doubleSnake", "from": newPosition, "to": finalPosition }
                    : { type: "snake", "from": newPosition, "to": finalPosition }
            }

            // Save Position and Last Roll
            await UPDATE(Players).set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })

            // Emit domain event so subscribers can react 
            if (event) await this.emit("BoardEvent", { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

            // Use req.reply(), instead of plain 'return', to pin the response value before CAP's
            // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
            // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
            req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? "normal" })

        })

        // ── confirmMove 
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player
        // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
        this.on("confirmMove", "Players", async (req) => {
            //For now, CAP framework handles the state change
        })

        // ── blockPlayer 
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
        // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
        this.on("blockPlayer", "Players", async (req) => {
            // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
            // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
            req.info("Player is blocked and will skip next turn")
        })

        // ── unblockPlayer 
        // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
        // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
        this.on("unblockPlayer", "Players", async (req) => {
            // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
            // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
        })

        // ── winGame 
        // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
        // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
        this.on("winGame", "Players", async (req) => {
            const player = SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
            if (!player) return req.error(404, "Player not found")

            // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
            await UPDATE("snakeladder.GameSessions").set({ finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(), winner_ID: player.ID }).where({ ID: player.ID })

            await this.emit("GameWon", { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
        })

        // -- skipTurn
        // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
        // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
        this.on("skipTurn", "Players", async (req) => {
            const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
            await UPDATE(Players)
                .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
                .where({ ID: player.ID })
        })

        return super.init()
    }
}

2. HTTP 409 Conflict

3. Player landing on a ladder

4. Prematurely calling 'winGame' action marks the player as winner without validating its position. I have not added any validation for this as of now, I assume that we call the 'winGame' action as a side-effect of 'rollDice' when a player reaches position 100.

entry.title: Setting Utilization % as low as 30-40% on some work centers to match real output — is this the right
entry.content:
System: SAP ECC 6.0, EHP8

Setting Utilization % as low as 30-40% on some work centers to match real output — is this the right use of the field?

We're standardizing how we set work center capacity across a multi-site manufacturing operation — eight to ten distinct processing types, hundreds of work centers company-wide and thousands of material masters -  I'm really struggling to accept the new approach and believe it should be challenged before it's locked in everywhere.

The directive coming down: set every work center's shift capacity at around 7 standard hours (based on an 8.5-hour shift, less standard breaks), the directive is to leave Length of Break at zero, and leave Cap. Utilization % at 100 — across the board, regardless of what a work center actually produces. There's a fairly firm belief behind this that setting utilization to anything other than 100% will cause problems with scheduling though I haven't seen that pinned down to anything specific yet. I firmly believe breaks and utilization % are fields to be used in CR12 and only affects available capacity which finite scheduling with fill and level against.

 

What I'm proposing instead: keep Start/End Time and breaks reflecting the real 8.5-hour shift schedule, and use Cap. Utilization % to scale capacity down to match each work center's demonstrated output — a rolling 30-60 day average of what it actually produces.

 

One methodology note: demonstrated output or historical confirmed std hours per day would be pulled from work centers and processing types that were fully loaded during that window — i.e., not idle or underutilized for other reasons — so the number reflects a real ceiling, not a slow period. That said, we know this won't land perfectly for every single work center as there are many variables that affect those work centers (machines) ability to output at the desired targets. It's meant as a defensible target to set capacity against and refine over time, not a one-time precise measurement.

 

Here's the part I expect will raise eyebrows: on some work centers, that means setting utilization as low as 30-40%. The gap between theoretical shift capacity and real output on certain machines is that large, driven by a mix of things — newer employees still ramping up, machine reliability issues, standard labor calculation errors either in formulas or MM specific std labor, general staffing levels at machines for loading and unloading, product mix and plain inefficiency. None of those are shift-length problems, so a flat number applied to every work center regardless of these differences doesn't fix any of them — it just hides them behind a ceiling that they are currently not or may never reach.

 

One more piece of context: we're also running a parallel initiative to improve actual labor capture at order confirmation as this has never been done to validate many labor estimates or old formulas. Up to now, actual hours haven't been recorded consistently — confirmations typically default to standard, which means we've had limited real data to separate a genuine performance gap from an inaccurate standard. That data is going to get better, but the capacity setup shouldn't wait on it — the output data we do have (from production reporting, independent of confirmation defaults) already shows the gap clearly enough to act on.

My questions for the community:

  1. Is using Utilization % this way — scaled per work center to align set capacity with demonstrated/capable output — the correct approach? Or is there a better mechanism intended for this?
  2. Is there a real technical basis for the belief that utilization below 100% causes problems? Or is that more of an organizational convention than an actual system constraint?

 

What I'm trying to protect: visibility of bottlenecks, a capacity that reflects what a work center can actually do, and orders that get produced and shipped on time. A standard capacity that very few work centers can hit defeats all three — it generates ATP dates against capacity that isn't there, and backlog builds invisibly until it's a missed delivery instead of an early warning.

 

Looking for real-world experience: has anyone set utilization this low on specific work centers, and did it hold up over time, especially across a large number of work centers and processing types? Any downsides I should know before challenging the current directive?

entry.title: Solution order revenue recognition
entry.content:
Hello experts.

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition using the Solution Order scope item, and we have the following business scenario:

A sales order is created for the sale of an engine. At the same time, a service order is created to plan and execute the installation of that engine.

The sales order is processed completely through billing, resulting in the corresponding accounting document. Meanwhile, the service order is used to plan the work and record the consumption of materials.

The business requirement is that the service order should not be billed separately, and that all costs incurred in the service order should be assigned to the sales document.

However, the standard functionality does not allow this type of service order to be settled to a cost object. Therefore, we would like to understand if by linking both the sales order and the service order to the same Solution Order, the revenue from the sales document is automatically associated with the costs recorded in the service order for profitability and reporting purposes.

We have also reviewed the analytical apps for Solution Orders, but we do not see any revenue or costs being reported. Therefore, we are wondering whether an additional process in Controlling (CO) or Financial Accounting (FI) is required to make this information available.

We have completed the entire 4GT test script, with the exception of billing the service order, since the business requirement is that the service order must not generate revenue. It should only record costs, while the only revenue should come from the sales order.

 

Thank you in advanced.

Best regards.

entry.title: Re: S-users Manage Their Own Cloud Notification Subscript...
entry.content:
egarding the question: "Which cloud notification subscriptions are applicable to our current SAP landscape?"

Please refer to the responses below:
  1. The cloud Production email notifications your company is eligible to subscribe to depend on two criteria:

    • Whether your company holds a valid contract for the respective product. To view your contracts, follow the steps to navigate to Finance & Legal -> Cloud -> Contracts.
    • Whether the product supports subscriptions via the Get Notified application. For detailed information, please refer to KBA 3164317 – Which cloud products are available in the Manage Cloud Service Availability Notifications? – SAP for Me.

    There is also a simpler way to verify eligible products for subscription. Ask a Cloud Administrator with sufficient authorizations to access the Get Notified application. The cloud services visible for selection in line with the screenshot represent all cloud products your company can subscribe to.

  2. As for which cloud notification subscriptions should be enabled for our S-users, this is determined by your company’s operational requirements. As SAP Technical Support, we are unable to assess your business needs and provide recommendations. Please select the subscribed products based on your own requirements.

entry.title: From Stakeholder Alignment to Inventory Optimization: What’s New in SAP Signavio Value Accelerators
entry.content:
Successful transformation depends on more than identifying opportunities for improvement. Organizations also need the ability to align stakeholders, prioritize actions, and execute changes with confidence. This latest SAP Signavio release introduces new capabilities designed to support both sides of that equation: helping teams collaborate more effectively around value accelerator adoption while uncovering actionable opportunities to optimize inventory performance.

From evaluating and selecting the right value accelerators to identifying excess inventory and improving safety stock settings, these enhancements help organizations turn insights into measurable business outcomes. The new downloadable content overview enables administrators, process owners, and business stakeholders to collaborate more efficiently around deployment decisions, creating a shared understanding of available value accelerator content without requiring direct access to the system. At the same time, a new inventory optimization value accelerator helps supply chain teams gain visibility into potential excess stock, prolonged inventory coverage, and safety stock settings that may no longer reflect business needs.

Read on to explore what’s new for this July release:

Value Accelerator Library

Value Accelerator Content Overview Download

Adopting a value accelerator is often a collaborative decision that involves more than just system administrators. Process owners, business stakeholders, and transformation teams all need visibility into the available content before deciding what should be deployed. To support these discussions, SAP Signavio now provides a downloadable content overview directly from the value accelerators library.

This enhancement gives teams an easy way to share accurate and up-to-date information about a value accelerator beyond the confines of the application. Instead of relying on manually maintained documentation or screenshots that can quickly become outdated, administrators can generate a comprehensive overview document and distribute it to stakeholders for review. By making value accelerator content accessible offline, organizations can engage a broader audience in the evaluation process, including stakeholders who may not have direct access to the system. This enables more informed discussions, simplifies cross-functional collaboration, and helps teams align more quickly on which accelerators best support their transformation objectives.

The result is a smoother decision-making process with a single source of truth that can be easily shared across teams. By reducing the effort required to communicate and review accelerator content, organizations can move from evaluation to deployment faster while ensuring that all key stakeholders are aligned along the way.

Value Accelerator Content Overview Download

Supply Chain

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Excess Stock and Safety Stock Levels

Maintaining the right inventory levels is a constant balancing act. Excess stock ties up working capital and consumes valuable warehouse space, while inaccurate safety stock settings can obscure opportunities for optimization. To help organizations identify and address these challenges, SAP Signavio introduces a new value accelerator focused on inventory optimization and safety stock management.

Built for supply chain and inventory management teams, this accelerator provides a dedicated improvement opportunity dashboard in SAP Signavio Process Insights that highlights material and plant combinations with potentially excessive inventory levels or safety stock settings that may no longer reflect actual demand patterns.

The dashboard helps teams quickly identify inventory situations that warrant further investigation, including materials with exceptionally high stock coverage and prolonged days of supply. It also surfaces cases where stock levels fall below defined safety stock thresholds despite available inventory already covering replenishment lead times, revealing potential opportunities to reassess safety stock definitions and planning parameters.

Key improvement opportunities include:

  • Material and plant combinations with 999 days of supply and available stock
  • Material and plant combinations with more than 180 days of supply
  • Material and plant combinations where stock is below the defined safety stock level but stock coverage exceeds replenishment lead time
  • Material and plant combinations where stock exceeds the defined maximum stock level for reorder point planning materials

By translating inventory and planning data into actionable insights, the accelerator helps organizations identify excess stock, improve inventory policies, and make more informed planning decisions. The result is better alignment between inventory levels and actual business needs, enabling companies to reduce unnecessary stock, free up warehouse capacity, and release working capital that can be invested elsewhere in the business.

Improvement Opportunity Dashboard to Reduce Excess Stock and Safety Stock Levels

Conclusion

This latest SAP Signavio release not only helps organizations uncover new improvement opportunities but also makes it easier to collaborate on and act upon them. By introducing downloadable value accelerator content overviews and a new inventory optimization accelerator, SAP Signavio supports both better decision-making and more effective execution across transformation initiatives.

The downloadable content overview simplifies how teams evaluate and adopt value accelerators by providing a shareable, up-to-date source of information that can be reviewed by administrators, process owners, and business stakeholders alike. At the same time, the new inventory optimization accelerator helps supply chain teams identify excess stock, reassess safety stock settings, and uncover opportunities to free up working capital through data-driven insights.

Together, these enhancements reinforce SAP Signavio’s commitment to accelerating process transformation with practical, business-focused capabilities. Whether organizations are aligning stakeholders on accelerator adoption or identifying opportunities to optimize inventory performance, SAP Signavio continues to help teams move from insight to action faster, enabling more informed decisions, stronger cross-functional collaboration, and measurable business outcomes.

 

To check out all the new accelerators mentioned in this blog post, access the value accelerator library which is embedded into SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite. For all SAP Signavio Process Insights value accelerators, you can access them directly through the dashboard. 

Find the value accelerator library in the side panel of SAP Signavio Process Collaboration Hub and get started today!

If you’re interested in learning more about the rest of the SAP Signavio solutions, check out the blog posts below:

If you’re interested in learning more about the rest of the SAP Signavio solutions, check out the blog posts below:

  • SAP Signavio July 2026 Release Highlights webcast
  • Process and Journey Modeling
  • AI-assisted process modeler
  • Data Driven Process Simulation

entry.title: Variable pay for Bonus calculation Equation custom formula
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am using a custom formula in the Basis Calculation Equation to calculate different basis amounts depending on whether an employee is on Leave of Absence (LOA), since the bonus calculation is prorated based on the employee's hire date and the time spent on LOA.

To support this requirement, I mapped the LOA information from a custom field in Job Information to Employee History. However, after making this change and trying to perform the Import Employee History from Employee Central, the job fails every time with the following error:

"This scheduled job failed to complete. For details, please contact Technical Support.."

To troubleshoot the issue, I performed the following tests:

  • If I keep the IF condition but replace the custom LOA field with a standard field (for example, Country), the job completes successfully, and Employee History is imported without any issues.

  • If I remove the IF condition and leave only a single calculation in the formula, the job also completes successfully.

Has anyone experienced similar behavior? Are there any known limitations when using custom Job Information fields in a Basis Calculation Equation, or is there any additional configuration required to make custom fields available in these formulas?

Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.content: The easiest way to do this is to use Export Master Data - Business Partner , update, and then use Mass Maintenance Business Partner to go through update process.  Note the only real trick is that there is a hidden column A which you need to update with “U” or “I” for update or insert respectively.  Plenty of documentation on this process in the Fiori Apps library, SAP documentation, or even YouTube (I would suspect)

entry.title: Re: Mass change of Manage flexible workflow settings
entry.content:
@Naveenk_S42024 If there were lingering, items not approved, then use this app to reassign them 

Flexible WorkFlow Administration 

If you have 900 workflows containing this user or custom business objects, I’d suggest using the Manage Teams and Responsibilities and redesigning workflows for future maintenance, instead of hardcoding users.  There are released CDS views for Teams that can be used to blend with custom development if required. 

I can’t imagine any business would have 900+ workflows referencing a user, but please reward points if helpful

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Financial Accounting
entry.content:
Business Scenario

As Bike Company, we serve multiple customers across various segments. However, we are 

aware that one particular customer has a history of delayed payments.

A dunning process should be in place for cases where invoices become overdue to ensure 

timely follow-up and improve receivables management. Should the payment deadline be 

missed, the system is configured so that the customer immediately receives a dunning notice 

at level 1 upon completion of the days of arrears period.

This automated process supports proactive receivables control while maintaining 

professional and consistent communication with our customers.

To implement this in the system, you need to perform the following steps:

1. Create a customer invoice:

Generate a customer invoice for customer S4C273, amounting to € 1,273, dated with 

today’s invoice date. The invoice should be set as due immediately. Please ensure that you 

use Company Code 1010, G/L Account 41000500, and Tax Code A0 for the posting.

After posting the document, make a note of the document number for reference.

2. Prepare and execute a dunning run for your customer S4C273:

Use an existing dunning procedure for your Business Partner S4C273 that applies a 

dunning charge of €10 at dunning level 1 on the dunning notice. Please don’t modify 

existing dunning procedures or create a new one.

When analyzing and selecting the dunning procedure, consider the following information 

for the subsequent dunning run:

● Check the number of days in arrears in your dunning procedure for dunning level 1. 

Then, execute the dunning run on the following date. After the dunning run, this date 

must be visible in the Last Dunning Date field in the business partner master record.

● Use the app Create Dunning Notices for your dunning run. Enter 273 as identification. 

Please restrict the dunning run exclusively to company code 1010, customer S4C273, 

and the invoice created in step 1. The date in the Documents Posted Up To Date field 

must match the dunning date.

Kindly help in configuring this scenario.
